# Scenario 3: Dependency Management (Platform/BOM) (What to Do When a Platform Aligns Versions — Until Something Overrides It)

## What This Scenario Demonstrates
This scenario demonstrates how to use Maven BOMs in Gradle using `platform()`.

## Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] -- platform --> BOM[demo-bom:1.0.0]
    Root --> LibA[lib-a]
    Root --> LibB[lib-b]
```

### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-3-mgmt/build.gradle" snippet="s3-deps"}
```

## Expected Intuition
"I import the BOM, so I don't need versions."

## Actual Resolution Results
*   **Gradle**: Resolves versions from the BOM.

## Classpath Reality
Versions match the BOM.

## Why This Happens
`platform()` imports the BOM as a set of dependency constraints.

## How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
N/A

## Safer Ways to Take Control
Use `enforcedPlatform()` if you want to force these versions (like `<dependencyManagement>` in Maven) rather than just recommend them.

## Signals to Watch For
N/A

## How This Scales in Real Systems
Standard practice for Spring Boot and other large ecosystems.

## Key Takeaway
Gradle treats BOMs as platforms.

## Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 4: Override](../scenario-4-override/guide.qmd)
